text: The surgeon begins the procedure by exposing the pancreas, preparing for the resection phase of the Whipple procedure.

The surgeon has reached the pancreas, lifting the yellow pancreatic tissue to expose the underlying portal vein. The purple structure of the portal vein is being carefully dissected away from the back of the pancreas.
The surgeon assesses the portal vein's adherence to the pancreas, confirming it is not too adherent, allowing the procedure to continue.
The surgeon retracts the stomach to reveal the first portion of the small intestine, known as the duodenum, and begins freeing the surrounding tissue to facilitate its removal.
The surgeon separates the duodenum from the surrounding posterior tissue, preparing for its removal as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy.

The surgeon identifies the right kidney located behind the duodenum and continues to divide the attachments between the duodenum and the retroperitoneum, separating the duodenum from the surrounding tissue in the back of the abdomen.
The surgeon disconnects surrounding tissue to free the duodenum, preparing it for removal along with the head of the pancreas, which is adherent to the duodenum.
The surgeon continues to separate the attachments between the duodenum and the retroperitoneum, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue to facilitate removal.

The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava, a large vein located in the retroperitoneum behind the duodenum. The surgical team proceeds to separate the connections between the duodenum and the vena cava.

The surgeon begins the portal dissection, focusing on the portal triad or porta hepatis, which is visible at the top left of the screen. These structures lead from the small intestine up towards the liver.

The surgical field reveals the portal vein, common bile duct, and hepatic artery. The surgeon ensures blood flow to the liver by preserving the hepatic artery and portal vein, while preparing to divide the common bile duct, which passes through the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the right gastric artery, a vessel extending from the hepatic artery towards the duodenum and pancreas, and prepares to divide it as part of the pancreaticoduodenectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the hepatic artery, which is visible in the surgical field. Lymph nodes adjacent to the hepatic artery are being carefully dissected and removed to ensure accurate cancer staging.

The surgeon removes lymph nodes to improve cancer staging and clear the surgical field, revealing the hepatic artery and its branches. The gastroduodenal artery is identified as it descends towards the duodenum. The surrounding tissue is meticulously dissected to skeletonize the artery, allowing for clear visualization and preparation for division.

The surgeon isolates the gastroduodenal artery and clamps it, using a special camera to visualize the liver's perfusion with injected ICG dye, confirming safe division of the artery while maintaining blood flow to the liver.

The surgeon uses clips to ligate and scissors to divide the gastroduodenal artery. The procedure progresses to maneuver around the stomach and duodenum, performing a pyloric preserving Whipple procedure, which involves leaving the entire stomach intact.

The surgeon has divided the stomach and pushes it aside to access the portal vein. The pancreas, shaped like a fish with a head, body, and tail, is lifted off the portal vein at the neck region using umbilical tape. This tape is used to elevate the pancreas, marking the area for division. The portion of the pancreas to the left of the division will be removed, while the section to the right will remain functional.

The surgeon locates the common bile duct at the portohepatous and isolates it for division, revealing a replaced right hepatic artery behind it, which is preserved to maintain blood flow to the right liver. Attention is then turned to the small bowel, identifying the duodenum's exit from the pancreas, where the small bowel will be divided.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the attachments of the small bowel, ensuring it is free for reconstruction. The bowel to the right of the stapler remains in the patient and will be brought up to the pancreas and bile duct for reconnection, maintaining normal flow of bile and pancreatic juice to the stomach.

The surgeon pulls the small bowel across to the opposite side of the abdomen, preparing to divide the pancreas. The pancreas is visibly separated from surrounding structures, and the division process begins.
The surgeon uses scissors and cautery to divide the pancreas completely, minimizing bleeding. The pancreas is then rolled off the portal vein. The surgical focus shifts back to the bile duct.

The surgeon divides the bile duct while carefully preserving the replaced right hepatic artery. Using a hook, they isolate a branch from the portal vein, applying a clip before dividing it with a vessel sealer. The pancreas is further separated from the portal vein, which transitions into the superior mesenteric vein. The first jejunal branch is identified wrapping behind the portal vein, and smaller branches are selectively divided.

The surgeon carefully separates the pancreas from the superior mesenteric artery and portal vein, ensuring the specimen is completely freed. The specimen is then placed into an endo-pouch for safe removal and sent to pathology for margin assessment.

The surgeon prepares for reconstruction by bringing the previously divided small bowel up behind the colon into the right abdomen, where it will be reconnected to the pancreas, bile duct, and stomach.

The surgeon positions the small bowel adjacent to the pancreas and bile duct, preparing for anastomosis. The first anastomosis involves connecting the pancreas to the small bowel by opening the bowel and inserting the cut end of the pancreas into it.

The surgeon performs a pancreatic jejunal invagination by sewing the pancreas to the small bowel. The technique varies depending on the thickness of the pancreas and the size of the pancreatic duct, with options including duct mucosa anastomosis.

The surgeon opens the small bowel, creating an incision approximately the same size as the pancreas. The cut end of the pancreas is then dunked into the small bowel, allowing pancreatic fluid to drain into the small intestine. The peristaltic movement of the small intestine facilitates the flow of pancreatic juice towards the stomach, where it will mix with ingested food.

The surgeon completes the back row of sutures and begins the front row, securing the pancreas to the bowel to facilitate drainage into the bowel. The anastomosis is carefully constructed to minimize the risk of pancreatic fluid leakage, with a drain placed post-surgery to manage any potential leaks.

The surgeon completes sewing the front wall of the anastomosis between the pancreas and the small bowel, finalizing the pancreatic anastomosis. The procedure then progresses to the anastomosis between the common bile duct and the small bowel.

The surgeon ensures there is no tension in the connection between the small bowel and the common bile duct, positioning the bowel to reach the duct easily. The gallbladder is removed as part of the Whipple procedure, since its function is compromised due to the removal of the duodenum.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to detach the gallbladder from the liver, isolating the cystic duct and cystic artery. The cystic artery is clipped and divided, followed by the cystic duct, which is clipped twice on the bile duct side and once on the gallbladder side before removal.

The surgeon has removed the gallbladder and proceeds with the anastomosis between the common bile duct and the small bowel. Sutures are used to create the connection, with careful technique to minimize the risk of leakage. A drain is placed near the anastomosis to manage any potential leaks.

The surgeon completes the bile duct anastomosis and proceeds to perform the anastomosis between the stomach and the small intestine. The connection is made just beyond the pylorus muscle to preserve its function, allowing the stomach to empty normally.

The surgeon removes the staple line from the divided duodenum, revealing the pylorus muscle from the inside. The stomach is then sewn to the small bowel in a two-layer technique, addressing both the back and front walls to minimize the risk of leakage.

The surgeon completes the first layer of sutures on the front wall of the stomach-small intestine anastomosis, adding a few stitches for the second layer. After finishing the anastomosis, a drain is placed near the pancreas and bile duct anastomosis to manage potential leaks.
